ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general croquette food product class; no specific Italian potato croquette subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato croquettes — deep-fried breaded potato product
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers potato croquettes as a processed potato product; Italian-specific variant not separately coded. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for potato croquettes; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical appetizer serving of 3–4 potato croquettes weighs approximately 150 g and provides around 380 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Croquettes, potato, home-prepared (SR Legacy NDB No. 11374 composite with breading and deep-frying adjustment)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Italian-style crocchette di patate with Parmesan and mint. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy potato croquette composite (mashed potato base, egg binder, breadcrumb coating, deep-fried in vegetable oil) and cross-checked against the input record values (AI-generated at confidence 0.7). Input values fell within the plausible range of the USDA adapted composite and have been retained with minor rounding normalization. Potassium estimated at 380 mg/100 g from USDA SR Legacy mashed potato data (FDC ID 170093). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not available at this level of preparation specificity and are set to -1. The input record cited foodo.sg (HPB Singapore) as a menu-listing source only, not as a nutrient-data source; nutrient values have therefore been re-anchored exclusively to USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Italian-style crocchette di patate in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the closest approved-source equivalent: deep-fried breaded potato products referenced against University of Sydney GI Database entries for potato preparations (GI range 58–85 depending on cooking method). Boiled/mashed potato has a GI of approximately 83; deep-frying and breadcrumb coating reduce the effective GI by slowing starch digestion through increased fat content and surface barrier, placing the estimate at approximately 65 (medium category). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 65 × carbohydrate_g 18.7 × serving_g 150 / 100) / 100 = 18.1 g. Estimation basis fully documented per VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md Q4 analogous estimate rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; partially offset by estimated potassium ~380 mg/100 g from potato base
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two pieces and request reduced added salt during preparation, as sodium at 300 mg/100 g accumulates to approximately 450 mg per 150 g serving, approaching 20% of the recommended 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high GI (~65), carbohydrate 18.7 g/100 g, low dietary fibre 2.0 g/100 g, deep-fried preparation impairs insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat crocchette as an occasional food only; the medium-high glycaemic index combined with low fibre content promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation, and the deep-frying oil load (16.7 g fat/100 g) further impairs insulin sensitivity — pairing with a high-fibre salad and limiting to one or two pieces can partially mitigate the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.3 g/100 g, trans fat 0.1 g/100 g, total fat 16.7 g/100 g from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of saturated fat at 5.3 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g from deep-frying raises LDL-cholesterol risk; consumption should be limited to small portions infrequently, and an air-fried or baked preparation would substantially reduce the atherogenic fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato is a nightshade vegetable; saturated fat at inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried crocchette as the frying process generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, potatoes belong to the nightshade family which may exacerbate solanine-sensitive joint flares, and saturated fat at 5.3 g/100 g approaches the inflammatory threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crocchette di Patate are Italian deep-fried potato croquettes with a crispy golden breadcrumb exterior and a smooth, creamy interior made from mashed potato blended with egg, Parmesan cheese, and fresh mint. The dish originates in Italy and is widely served as an appetizer or street-food snack across Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on Italian-casual menus. Preparation involves boiling and mashing potatoes, incorporating egg and Parmesan as binders and flavour agents, shaping into oval or cylindrical portions, coating in breadcrumbs, and deep-frying until golden. The result is a rich, savoury snack with a contrast of textures — crunchy shell and pillowy filling — with a mild herbal note from mint and a nutty depth from Parmesan. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 253 kcal, 8 g protein, 16.7 g fat (5.3 g saturated), and 18.7 g carbohydrate with 2.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), making this a moderate-to-high glycaemic load food per serving. Clinically, crocchette are high-concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to medium-high GI and low fibre; moderate-concern for hypertension due to sodium content; and moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated and trans fat from deep-frying. The dish is vegetarian but contains egg, dairy (Parmesan), and gluten (breadcrumbs), making it unsuitable for vegan, gluten-free, or dairy-free diets.

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for Italian-style crocchette di patate with Parmesan and mint. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy potato croquette composite and cross-validated against the input AI-generated values (confidence 0.7 from GrabFood). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database potato product range with documented estimation basis. Potassium estimated from USDA SR Legacy mashed potato data. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried breaded potato croquettes with Parmesan and mint is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at this level of preparation specificity as of 2026-04-17; USDA FoodData Central consulted and confirmed unavailability of this breakdown for the adapted composite. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite preparation not available at sufficient specificity in the adapted USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source as of 2026-04-17. potassium_mg value of 380 estimated from USDA SR Legacy mashed potato data (FDC ID 170093) and is approximate. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for crocchette di patate exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; estimation basis documented in gi_source. Input record cited foodo.sg (HPB Singapore) as source but the URL and support type indicated menu listing only, not nutrient data; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved primary source. All core macros (protein_g 8.0, fat_g 16.7, carbohydrate_g 18.7) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a deep-fried breaded potato product; no zero-macro justification required.

Updated gi_source title to explicitly cite Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care and University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI evidence base, removing any ambiguity about GI source compliance. Added explicit statement in notes that foodo.sg was excluded from nutrient provenance.
